content.assessment: T3 is the proposal most aligned with the lens's central logic: it explicitly treats improvement as a bundle (pedagogical networks, multi-service hubs, multigrade pedagogy, kindergarten-primary co-provision) rather than a single lever, echoing the whole-system reform principle that gains require coordinated, mutually reinforcing measures rather than isolated interventions. Its pedagogical design draws on cross-national evidence (Chile, Norway) showing multigrade/mixed-age models can achieve quality only when supported by deliberate teacher training and professional networks (F-live-portuguese_reform-07) — a genuinely non-structural, capacity-building lever consistent with the lens's first question. However, the proposal's own risk section concedes that its own template case (Erdőkürt) succeeded due to rare local social capital (F-live-portuguese_reform-10), which is precisely the same non-structural, unevenly distributed resource that undermines T1. This creates an internal tension: T3 bundles structural network design with an implicit reliance on the same social-capital variable that the lens flags as a source of inequitable, non-generalizable outcomes, meaning quality levelling across villages is not guaranteed by network design alone.
opportunities: Systematic teacher-training and cross-school professional-network investment could substitute for reliance on organic social capital, converting an uneven asset into a designed, equitable system feature
strengths: Explicitly bundles multiple coordinated levers (pedagogical networks, multi-service hubs, kindergarten-primary integration) rather than relying on one isolated mechanism, consistent with lens principle that gains are attributable to bundles not single interventions

Draws on cross-national evidence that multigrade teaching can achieve quality when paired with deliberate teacher training and professional learning communities, a scalable non-structural quality lever (F-live-portuguese_reform-07)
threats: Without deliberate national investment replicating the Chile/Norway training-and-network conditions, cluster quality may default to the uneven, capital-dependent pattern already observed (F-live-portuguese_reform-10)
weaknesses: The proposal's core viability may still depend on the same rare, unevenly distributed local social capital documented in the single Erdőkürt case, undermining claims of scalable equity levelling (F-live-portuguese_reform-10)
Evidence for multigrade/network pedagogical quality comes from non-Hungarian systems (Chile, Norway), leaving causal attribution to the Hungarian context unverified (F-live-portuguese_reform-07)
